_citation.full_reference: Rao, S. R. 1982. The Decipherment of the Indus Script. New Delhi: Asia Publishing House. ISBN 0-7069-1791-1. Pp. 384.
additional_sources: Rao, S. R. 1991. Dawn and Devolution of the Indus Civilization. New Delhi: Aditya Prakashan.
Rao, S. R. 1973. Lothal and the Indus Civilization. Bombay: Asia Publishing House.
_citation.license: Synthetic reconstruction of S.R. Rao's Sanskrit-logographic competing hypothesis for Phase-30c T3-v2 falsification testing. Rao identifies Indus signs with Old Indo-Aryan / Vedic Sanskrit lexical items, proposing the script encodes a pre-Vedic Aryan dialect. Reference use only.
_citation.compiled_by: Glossa-Lab Phase-30c T3-v2 (synthetic Rao Sanskrit-logographic map).

_doc: S.R. Rao Sanskrit-logographic competing phoneme map. Rao 1982 proposed that the Indus script is alphabetic-syllabic and encodes Old Indo-Aryan, with the most frequent signs being phonetic syllables and the iconic signs being logograms for Vedic concepts (yajna 'sacrifice', soma 'ritual drink', agni 'fire', etc.). This is a SYNTHETIC reconstruction giving Rao's hypothesis maximum benefit of the doubt for the iconographic-anchor test by mapping each anchored sign to its most plausible Vedic Sanskrit lexical equivalent.
_methodology_note: Rao's actual claim is mixed alphabetic-syllabic (he claims a 20-character primary script with iconic logograms). To compare against Parpola's iconographic-anchor methodology, I have reconstructed the LOGOGRAPHIC subset of his readings, focusing on the 12 anchored signs. For 'fish' he proposes 'tūla' (Sanskrit star/asterism); this gives Rao's hypothesis a partial advantage on fish-star homonymy. Other readings come from Rao 1982 chapters 7-9 directly.

47.source: Rao 1982 ch. 8: 'fish' sign as tūla = star/asterism. Note this is Rao's strongest anchor — he claims fish-star polysemy via Sanskrit 'tūla'.
47.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'tūla' has minor astronomical use (lunar mansion under Libra) but is NOT a general 'star' word. The fish-star homonymy is much weaker in Sanskrit than in Dravidian *miin*.

311.gloss: fig/banyan; Vedic 'vaṭa' = banyan (note: Dravidian loanword in Sanskrit)
311.homonymy_note: Sanskrit borrowed 'vaṭa' from Dravidian (DEDR 5217). Rao using it for fig is fine, but 'vaṭa = north' homonymy is purely Dravidian, not Sanskrit.

117.gloss: wheel; Vedic 'cakra' = wheel, sun-wheel, Visnu's discus
117.source: Rao 1982 ch. 9: cakra sign; central to Indus iconography

87.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'tāra' = 'star', so 'dvi-tāra' could mean 'two stars' but lacks the precise Old Tamil veN-miin (Venus = white star) idiom.

91.homonymy_note: Vedic 'kṛttikā' is the standard name for Pleiades; 'ṣaḍ-tāra' is constructed but not idiomatic Sanskrit.

92.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'saptarṣi' (Seven Sages) is the standard term, not 'sapta-tāra'.

175.gloss: spinning device; no firm Sanskrit anchor for radiance

261.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'rudra' / 'skanda' both work for war-god, but neither carries the 'ear-ring/bangle' homonymy that Dravidian *muruku* does.

281.source: Rao 1982: 'young' connotation but no squirrel cognate
281.homonymy_note: Sanskrit 'bāla' (child) lacks squirrel meaning; same disadvantage as Yajnadevam's 'putra'.

summary.claim_summary: Rao's hypothesis: Indus is Old Indo-Aryan / Vedic Sanskrit. Marginally stronger than Yajnadevam on fish-star (via 'tūla') and weaker on most other anchors.
